Iri presented the catalogue update: ninety-seven known wounded members, three silent-one vigils active, one healed member choosing permanent privacy, twelve anomaly traces under recurrence review. The numbers no longer moved the room toward triumph. They moved it toward posture. What kind of people would they be with ninety-seven remaining? With zero? With unknown wounds beyond the map?

Dren's testimony request remained open. He had not appeared. He had sent three clarifying questions and one correction to the public record about Mava Sorn's final remnant, which had collapsed the previous winter after half its members accepted reception and the rest scattered north. Mava herself had died without testimony. Her record remained incomplete, which Senna insisted students learn without turning incompleteness into mystery.

At the observance, Ashe read a short statement from the newest badlands reception group.

`We were told the restoration would make us perform gratitude before it gave us water. It gave us water first. We do not know what to do with that.`

The hall sat with the sentence.

Then the entity transmitted the anniversary harmonic.

For the first time, Corin heard it without the Echo underneath.

He had expected the music to feel thinner. Instead it had depth he had never perceived because the broken connection had always been speaking through it. Lira's first twelve measures. The entity's adaptations. Student dissonances. Northern pressure rhythms. Silent-one intervals approved for public use. A faint space where the unresolved harmonic return still waited beyond the mapped network.

The symphony did not need his fracture to reach him.

He cried then, quietly enough that only Senna noticed and loudly enough that she passed him a cloth without mockery.

After the ceremony, Pella found him near the side doors.

"You look different," she said.

"Better?"

"Different. Do not make me sentimental."

"I would never."

She studied him with director's eyes, friend's eyes, witness eyes. "Are you still listening?"

The question did not hurt.

"Yes," he said. "Just not through a wound."

Pella nodded once. "Good."

That evening, the old group gathered in the garden, smaller now and larger at once. Corin, Senna, Pella, Jaeven, Iri when she arrived late from an operation, Ashe by remote from the third timeline, Sable in the lamps, the entity in the baseline, students drifting at the edges because Senna had stopped pretending the garden belonged only to history. They ate, argued, corrected memory, and let silences remain unfilled.

Near midnight, a message arrived from the unresolved harmonic return project.

Sable displayed it on the garden wall only after Pella gave permission.

`Boundary echo repeated during anniversary harmonic. Pattern suggests recognition rather than random resonance. Confidence remains low.`

No one rushed to interpret it.

That was new too.

The young composer who had written the movement Corin disliked asked, "Recognition of us?"

Iri answered carefully. "Recognition of pattern. Maybe. Not necessarily us."

"What pattern?"

The entity's presence warmed through the lamps.

*Staying.*

The word moved across the garden with almost no sound.

Corin thought of the first silent one. Pain changed and we remained. He thought of the Echo, fulfilled and gone. He thought of Aldric's unfinished question, Lira's living symphony, Senna's watering can in his hands. He thought of the last hundred becoming ninety-seven and of all the numbers that still hid names no one had permission to know.

At the edge of the garden, a child asked whether the echo beyond the network was lonely.

No adult answered quickly.

Finally Senna said, "We do not know. That is why we listen before deciding what our listening means."

The child accepted this with the solemn disappointment of someone denied a simpler story.

Later, after the others left, Corin remained by the silver-frond tree. He no longer waited for the Echo to return. The silence in him was not empty. It held the city, the symphony, the ordinary scrape of Senna stacking cups, the distant hum of the array, the possibility of an answer coming from beyond the known map.

Senna came beside him. "What happens now?"

The old question. The first question. The question that had survived every answer.

Corin looked at the garden he had finally been allowed to water.

"Now we stop treating endings like proof that the work mattered," he said.

Senna's mouth curved. "That is too long for a marker."

"You can edit."

"I always do."

They stood until the lamps dimmed to night mode.

The Echo was released.

The witness remained.

The next morning, Corin found a new marker in the garden.

Senna had placed it near the bed of aggressive pepper-scented plants from the retired healer's illegal rooftop garden. The plants had been formally admitted after a committee determined that memorial cultivation rules were less important than the fact that children loved touching the leaves and then sneezing dramatically. The marker was plain, written in Senna's hand.

`Presence is what remains when the message no longer needs the wound.`

Corin stared at it. "That is too long for a marker."

"I edited."

"From what?"

"You do not want to know."

He crouched and pressed the soil around its base. "This bed will overgrow it."

"Then someone will have to uncover it."

"Convenient metaphor."

"Useful gardening."

They worked until the sun cleared the wall. For once, no anniversary bell interrupted them. No crisis call came from the Corps. No echo sounded from beyond the network boundary, though the project continued listening. The day offered no proof of meaning except the work itself: water, soil, labels, weeds, the stubborn pepper smell clinging to Corin's sleeves.

At noon, a group of students arrived for Senna's advanced witness seminar. She made Corin stay. He objected that he had not prepared a lecture. Senna said preparation had always been his way of hiding behind usefulness and handed him a chair.

The students asked about the Echo.

Not politely. Young people trained by Senna rarely confused politeness with care.

"Do you miss it?"

"Yes," Corin said.

"Should we be sad it is gone?"

"Only if sadness helps you respect what it carried. Not if sadness makes you want it back."

"Was it worth it?"

He looked toward the garden wall, beyond which the city continued being ordinary with magnificent indifference.

"That question is too large for one life," he said. "But my part in it was worth continuing."

The students wrote that down. He hoped they would argue with it later.

When the seminar ended, one student stayed behind. She was human, perhaps sixteen, with a resonance sensitivity badge clipped to her sleeve. "I am afraid my Echo will break in the exam," she said.

Corin felt the old world lean close.

"Then we redesign the exam," Senna said from behind them.

The student blinked.

Corin turned to Senna, and both of them began laughing. Not because the fear was funny. Because for forty-two years they had been building a civilization where that answer could finally be obvious.

The witness remained, yes.

But so did revision.

That became the sentence Senna finally put on a shorter marker.

`Witness remains. Revision continues.`

She installed it beside the exam redesign notice after the policy passed six months later. No apprentice would ever again press a palm to a tuning plate without live consent withdrawal, witness support, and a curriculum that named Corin's old exam not as a heroic origin but as a failure no civilized school should repeat.

Corin attended the first redesigned exam.

The student with the resonance badge passed. Nothing shattered. No one died in projection. The room was almost boring.

Afterward, she asked if boring disappointed him.

"No," he said, meaning it more than he expected. "Boring is what we were trying to earn."

The student grinned. The room stayed ordinary and calm afterward, almost deliberately. Corin counted that as a success.
